skill.name: stack-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze a project technology stack, recommend matching AI development skills, and optionally scaffold project-specific skill templates.
skill.summary: Detect project technologies and recommend or scaffold matching AI development skills.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity weak-crypto hits, but review found those are false positives in prose, YAML labels, and schema text rather than cryptographic operations. The real risk is medium: the skill can guide an agent to copy templates and modify .claude/skills files in a target repository, so users should review paths and generated content before allowing scaffolding.

medium_findings.title: Scaffolding Commands Can Modify Target Repositories
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents shell commands that copy templates into a target repository and rewrite SKILL.md with cp, echo, command substitution, and sed. This is legitimate scaffolding behavior, but unsafe target paths or project names could cause unintended writes if executed without review. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The commands are shown directly in the skill workflow, but they are documentation instructions rather than executable bundled scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command sequence is explicit and could be executed by an agent. Risk depends on user approval and variable validation.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Hidden Skill Files May Be Created
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill is designed to write project-specific skills and a generated manifest under .claude/skills. This persistence is disclosed and useful, but generated instructions can affect future AI behavior and should be reviewed. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The hidden paths and manifest behavior are clearly described in the workflow and cleanup sections.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly names .claude/skills outputs and cleanup behavior. There is no evidence that persistence is hidden from the user.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The listed weak-cryptography hits occur in descriptions, headings, YAML recommendation metadata, or schema text. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API use. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: Manual review of the cited files found prose and configuration only, not code implementing weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are not cryptographic operations. The detector appears to have matched substrings or generic text.

low_findings.title: Network Finding Is A Documentation URL
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URL is the JSON Schema identifier inside an output schema example, not a fetch target or runtime network call. No evidence found of outbound requests. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The URL appears inside a markdown JSON example and is not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a schema URI in documentation. No network API or command references it.

low_findings.title: Template Static Hits Are Benign Placeholders
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The template findings point to placeholder tables, path examples, and sample test code. No evidence found of system reconnaissance, dynamic execution, or prompt injection attempts. Confidence: 0.9. Confidence reasoning: The template files contain project customization guidance and examples, not active command logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are markdown examples and tables. They do not instruct unsafe scanning or hidden execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Scaffolding Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes cp, echo, command substitution, and sed commands for scaffolding. Treat target paths and project names as untrusted inputs before execution. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The shell block is present and actionable, but no bundled script runs it automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in a bash block. Execution requires the agent or user to run them.

content.user_title: Analyze Project Stacks and Recommend Skills
content.value_statement: Teams often miss useful AI development skills when they onboard an existing repository. This skill maps detected technologies to recommended skills and can scaffold reviewed project-specific templates.
actual_capabilities: Invokes library-detection to identify languages, frameworks, test tools, databases, and build tools.
Matches detected technologies against YAML recommendation rules for generic skills.
Produces a stack analysis report with detected technologies and recommended skills.
Supports optional activation of recommended generic ai-dev-kit skills.
Can scaffold project-specific research, domain, and testing skill templates.
Documents generated skill files in a .claude/skills manifest for cleanup.
limitations: Detection quality depends on the separate library-detection skill output.
Scaffolded templates are placeholders and require project-specific review.
The skill does not validate every possible framework or custom build system.
Repository writes should be approved before scaffolding is allowed.

use_cases.title: Onboard an Existing Repository
use_cases.description: Detect the repository stack and produce a clear list of AI development skills to enable.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team AI Setup
use_cases.description: Use consistent recommendation rules so teams activate the same skills for similar technologies.

use_cases.title: Create Project-Specific Skill Drafts
use_cases.description: Scaffold editable skill templates for research, domain vocabulary, or custom testing workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze This Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use stack-analyzer to inspect this repository and produce a stack analysis report. Do not scaffold files.

prompt_templates.title: Recommend Skills Only
prompt_templates.prompt: Run stack-analyzer and list recommended generic skills with reasons. Ask before activating any skill.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Project Skill Scaffolding
prompt_templates.prompt: Use stack-analyzer to identify scaffold candidates. Show target paths and template names before writing files.

prompt_templates.title: Audit And Scaffold With Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run stack-analyzer with scaffolding planning enabled. Validate target paths, summarize file changes, and wait for approval before writing.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript and Python repository using React, FastAPI, Supabase, and Vitest.
output: Detected stack summary grouped by language, framework, database, and test tool.
Recommended generic skills for Supabase, React, FastAPI, testing, and security review.
Optional scaffold candidates with target paths for project-specific skills.

output_examples.input: A repository with research agents and domain models.
output: A recommendation to consider project-research and project-domain templates.
A short explanation of each trigger and what generated files would contain.
A reminder to review generated .claude/skills content before use.

output_examples.input: A request to set up AI skills without modifying files.
output: A report-only analysis with no repository writes.
Recommended skills marked as inactive until the user approves activation.

best_practices: Run in report-only mode before allowing activation or scaffolding.
Review all generated .claude/skills files before relying on them.
Keep recommendation rules aligned with the technologies your team actually supports.
anti_patterns: Do not scaffold into a repository without checking the target path first.
Do not treat template placeholders as final project knowledge.
Do not recommend skills for technologies that were not detected or confirmed.

faq.question: Does this skill run code by itself?
faq.answer: It provides workflow instructions and shell examples. Agents or users still control whether commands are executed.

faq.question: Can it modify my repository?
faq.answer: Yes, when scaffolding is approved. It can create project-specific skill files under .claude/skills.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes with a medium-risk warning. The main concern is controlled filesystem writes during scaffolding.

faq.question: Why were weak crypto findings dismissed?
faq.answer: The cited lines are descriptions, headings, and configuration text. No cryptographic implementation was found.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: When should I use report-only mode?
faq.answer: Use report-only mode for first review, audits, or any repository where file writes need approval.
